sentences.text: I was born in Hindenburg, Germany, which was the border town.
And we immediately went to Bedzin after I was born, because my father had a fruit store over there.
And my mother's sister was living there, also.
They also had a fruit store.
So we went right away to Bedzin, and my father remained-- we had a business in Hindenburg, in Germany, and I was raised in Bedzin.
We lived together with my grandfather and my mother.
And my father used to come home once a month, maybe every two weeks.
And he was living there.
That's because he was a woodturner and he couldn't make a living just with, just working with the wood.
So my mother's sister lived there, and they had a store, and they asked them to open a store there.
But then later, when Hitler came to power, he couldn't stay longer because we were all Polish citizens.
So he had to go home every week and then they make them go home every night.
He couldn't stay there.
He couldn't have a long visa.
He had to go home every night.
Finally it got so bad that they had to give up the store.
And my mother, in the meantime, opened up a store in Bedzin, of fruit and ice cream.
And then my father came back there, and we had the store.
And I went to school, into cheder, when I was young.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I remember when I was young, also, that sometimes I went also to Hindenburg.
I went back and forth, you know.
We, we came to Hindenburg, and they-- then my father came home.
That's how we were traveling together.
And then as I got bar mitzvah, we went to cheder, and I went to, to school with the [NON-ENGLISH] together, and the other friends.
Later, when the war broke out, everybody starts evacuating.
And I remember the 1st of September, they marched into Bedzin.
My father started to-- three days ago he walked away, he evacuated, because there was such a panic that they said that they're going to shoot the men.
So they went further east.
And when the Germans came in, right away, the next day, there were signs on the street-- anybody who has rifles or ammunition should bring it [INAUDIBLE] to the city hall.
And the second day, there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] signs, you know, all over on the doors-- anybody who has a radio should bring it in.
Or a knife, or anything-- we should bring it into the city hall.
And every, every day there was another order.
Later my father came back already, because the Germans got in fairly close.
The, the war was already over with Poland, so my father came back.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it-- the law started to be so that you cannot go on this side of the street.
The Jews can only go on one side of the street.
And then we had to move out from this part of the city to another part of the city.
And then we had to wear armbands on the hands with a Jewish star.
And then we had to-- every Jew had to work.
If you didn't work, they had special troops that they came into the town and they start grabbing people on the street.
So everybody was hiding.
Finally, they went to the Jewish, the Jewish community center there, and they demanded that they should give so many thousands of people to be transported, to be evacuated.
We did not know where they going to evacuate them.
We did not know that they take them to Auschwitz, and they're going to gas them.
We thought--
My grandfather from-- my mother's father-- died when he was 57 years old, so he died before the war.
But my father's father was also in Bedzin.
They were still there, but I was-- that's what I'm coming to.
When the Jewish community-- they made a, a special Jewish police because we were had to live on one part of side of the city.
We could not go on the streets, on the main streets, anymore.
We, we lived on the side of the city.
And there were Jewish police, where they was wearing white hats and a blue stripe around, and also a Jewish band with a Jewish blue star on it.
Later there came an order that we had to wear a yellow star on the, on the front, on the chest.
Yes, because they didn't trust-- because they didn't trust that some people took off the armband, and then they went on the Aryan side.
So, so we had to wear a Jewish star.

Two weeks later, a month later, we had to cut out a hole in the nicest suits that we had, and put the yellow star behind it, and sew it around so you cannot tear it off again, to make it sure for themselves.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In order to not to go to camp-- because they came with special commanders on the streets-- they came, maybe a few thousand soldiers.
And they started grabbing people from the street, whoever they see, on the trucks and sent them to camps or to Auschwitz.
In order not to go, you had to have a special pass, and they had blue-- some kind-- I had a, a card, a blue card.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] How did I get this blue card?
We used to live in a house-- there was-- after 7 o'clock we couldn't go out anymore.
So we lived in an apartment house where there was a neighbor.
He used to have an electrical store.
And after 7 o'clock in the evening, the neighbors got together.
There was nothing to do.
They talked politics, and they played cards.
So the neighbor-- we asked the neighbor to do a favor, maybe you can take him, my boy, into work.
So I went into work for him.
But he had a [GERMAN], they called it.
All the Jewish businesses were taken away from the Jews, and they put in a German in the business, that he is the whole manager for the business, and he was the boss.
And the owner worked for that boss now, for the, for the German.
So that [GERMAN] had three electrical stores, and through him I got into a store where I became an electrician.
And-- but by becoming electrician, I became-- I had a blue card, and on the blue card was written, from the SS, [SPEAKING GERMAN]
With this blue card, I was safe.
No, it's very hard for me to translate this.
It was a division that they took care for, for foreigners-- for foreign, for foreign war commanders in East Upper Silesia-- division of something from the SS.
With this card, I was safe, because other people had different colors of cards so they grabbed them away.
My father had a different color of a card, so they took him first to camp.
They grabbed him on, on the street, and he went to camp first, and I remained home with my mother and sister.
I think it was pink or red.
It meant that he was working-- but his card did not produce anything for the, for the German Wehrmacht, for the German army.
Electrical work was for material for the industry.
I was working at that time in an electrical store where they were only packing supplies and send it to Germany.
Electrical supplies-- and this was for German industry, going.
So I had a card that I was-- used-- it was more to the benefit for the Germans, because they needed me for the industry.
But later, and later on, they needed more people, where I went through a commission.
And they took me in that I'm going to be sent, but I was too young.
I was 16 years old.
So they sent me back, back to work there.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day-- every day the, the-- every time the German SS gave orders to the Jewish community center to give a quota of people to transport away.
So in the beginning they took the sick ones, the crazy ones, the people who were less, less educated.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Jewish police went door to door with a list.
They knocked on the door, middle of the night, because people would lock themselves up.
They didn't want to come.
So they went to the houses to, to grab them in the middle of the night.
One day they came for me in the middle of the night.
It was my turn already, because they-- it was less, and less, and less people.
Each time they demanded more, and if they were short, they took one policeman.
If they were short on the count, they took a policeman and put him on the truck.
So they came for, for me-- knocked on the door.
My mother opened up.
And my aunt was sleeping at that time-- also I was hiding under her cover.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was not here.
I heard that they were talking-- I was not here.
I was not in the house.
Where?
And my mother doesn't know where I am.
So they took my mother as a hostage, and they held her for a whole week in the basement in the Jewish center there, in the prison.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after a week they took her out to the truck and said, if you're not going to deliver your son, we're sending you to Auschwitz.
She was standing there, maybe a half hour, and I got the message that they're going to send her to Auschwitz.
I grabbed that little, that little suitcase-- what I had with me-- and I ran to the center there, and they sent her back home.
And I remember that she cried, and hugged me, and kissed me, and she cried so hard.
And she said, my child, my child, I see for the last time my child.
And I didn't see her anymore since that time.
That was in March 1943.
My father was already in camp, and then they took me.
No.
No.
We did not know what happened.
We know they sending people away, but we did not-- we had some kind of an idea.
We heard that they sending away trains, and then they changed the engines, and the, the Polish conductors came back.
They don't know where the trains are going.
We did not know exactly.
We didn't have any proof of anything.
Anyway, I was thrown there in the basement.
And from that time on, they-- I was laying there in the basement maybe five, six hours, and then they put me in a furniture truck.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A lot of people there, were there in the basement.
And they sent me to Sosnowitz.
Sosnowitz was like a prison there, and we were there maybe three, four days.
And then they sent us on a truck to Sacrow-- that was Germany already.
That was a transit camp.
We stayed in, in the camp [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] maybe a day.
And then the next five days, every day, they took me to work in the forest, to, to chop down trees.
And then we had to carry these trees on trucks.
It was very hard work.
And after five days we came back to camp.
They sent us to Sackenhoym.
That was a forced labor camp.
That was already the real camp, but I stayed there for a year.
There were no living conditions.
There was only a bed [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when you came home.
There was a bed.
I don't remember if there was any closets.
There was a--
Yes, with another one.
I was sleeping-- always shared with, with somebody else.
I sleep.
They sent us to Sackenhoym.
And that was already the real camp.
There they asked me for my-- for a trade.
Everybody said that he had a trade because he wanted to save his life.
Otherwise he had to work with the shovel.
If I would have to work with the shovel, I wouldn't be alive today.
So I was afraid myself.
I said I am an electrician.
I was very young.
What kind of electrician am I when I'm, I'm that young and I didn't have any schooling?
But I knew more, because I worked at home at the trade, than any, than the other people what they say they are electricians.
So they put me to work at the IG Farben industry that time.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The whole camp at Sackenhoym-- we marched every morning to the IG Farben industry.
Can you tell me a little bit about the IG Farben factory there?
The IG Farben factory-- they made the glycerine, chlorine, methanol, and the soap from stones, from coal.
They were burning coal.
And, and day and night there was pouring methanol and gas and went to the front.
They were chopping out forests and built these factories.
The factory was as big, like a quarter of the city of LA.
There were-- the streets were very wide.
Two tracks were on each side of this-- two railroad tracks could go on each side of the street, and in the middle there was room for traffic for, for trucks.
And there were giant bridges with pipes going in all directions-- vents and pipes connecting one building with the other.
There were buildings-- the same buildings built in parallel.
If one building gets bombed out, they closed the valve from this building, and the other building was working, and the gasoline was poured right away in the trains.
We worked in this camp a year, and later on when, when we worked at the camp we marched with the, with guards out.
And in the, in the, factory, inside, we were free, because they couldn't-- they didn't have so many guards to guard each electrician.
If they would work with a shovel in the hard work, they had a guard with them there all the time.
But with electricians, we, we had to come to the gate of the factory at the end of the day and wait for the whole colony, for the whole group, and then we marched into the camp.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I worked there with other-- two more guys.
And I was especially in the IG Farben-- in the [NON-ENGLISH] controller-- I worked.
[NON-ENGLISH] controller-- they kind of were in charge for the thermal couples and to see that the gas has the right temperature on the, on the towers.

[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And over there-- when we come home from work in the camp, they grabbed a few guys here and a few guys there to go unload trains with gravel after work or on a Saturday, to do this all day long.
Then I spoke to my boss.
Maybe you make me work-- I like to work Saturdays here.
I don't want to go into the camp.
He asked me why.
I tell him, because I-- it's no good in the camp.
We don't have any food there, and it's, it's hard work.
So the boss over there made me come in with the two other guys what they worked in the IG Farben industry, especially Saturday, to work.
The whole camp was-- did not work, and we three of us came in, and we worked Saturday.
But this was only a short time.
And when I worked there Saturday, there wasn't really too much work.
He got me to clean his bicycle, and he gave me a pot of soup.
He says, go wash that pot out, especially because he knew I didn't have any food.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Later on one day, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they came and they took the whole camp to the concentration camp, to Blechhammer.
In that camp, one guy ran away.
One guy didn't come back, because when we went out from the camp to the work, and came back in the camp, they counted us-- how many.
And there was one short.
He didn't come back.
So the whole group-- we were there, 50 people.
The whole group had to go into the barrack, and we got 25 on our ass.
And they were so brutal.
The 25 on the ass-- you had to kneel on a little school, and bend over on a chair, and drop your pants.

And there was one guy with a whip on this side, and the other guy on the other side, and they whipped you so hard that every time they whipped force on you behind, there was right a black-blue stripe on it that you-- they had to carry you out.
There was an incident that the father had to whip his son.
And he didn't whip him hard, so the German comes over there and says, come here, lay down here.
I'm going to show you how to whip.
And he got him real, real hard.
And if you're not going to do this, you're going to get it more.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] That's what it was, well, later on.
Well, only-- in this camp, only one ran away.
But in, in concentration camp, two more guys ran away, and we got it again.
I got it three times.
In this camp, yes.
I-- there was other people-- they got beaten, but I didn't witness this.
I didn't see it.
And then they took us later on to the concentration camp.
The whole camp was-- they put us on trucks, and we came into Blechhammer.
It's [NON-ENGLISH].
No, they didn't say nothing-- just go on the trucks, and that's it.
I don't know what they did.
I don't think so.
I think that camp was liquidated.
And every Jew went to concentration-- they made concentration-- it just had a different name.
But when we came into concentration camp there at Blechhammer, this was a camp before.
Before it was also a forced labor camp, but they made it a-- Blechhammer was commanded from Auschwitz.
It was only tradesmen.
Most of them were tradesmen-- construction people-- that they worked there.
And so they came and they gave me a tattoo, to Blechhammer.
We came into Blechhammer.
They shaved us.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They took everything away, whatever we had.
And they gave us these uniforms with the stripes, and wooden shoes.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the worst part was that when we had to stay on an appell-- they called it appell.
We had to get up early, very early in the morning, maybe 3, 4 o'clock in the morning.
It was night.
You had a cup of black coffee, and you had to stay until daylight appears.
And then you marched out to five.
When we marched out to five, we had to hold each other with locked in the hand to five, and march all the time.
And there was, with a gun, a guard on this side and a guard on this side.
And they were going-- every 50 feet, there was a guard like this.
And we marched-- 5,000 people-- we marched from Blechhammer to work every morning.
And then at the end of the day, we waited at the gate, and marched into camp-- back.
The march was maybe a half hour from camp.
And when the guards came in-- when we marched into the factory that was the [GERMAN], they called it.
It was so big that they were connected together with the IG Farben from the other side.
The guards came and they went apart, through the whole factory, and they guarded the whole factory-- the whole plant, they guarded.
When we marched in the, the [NON-ENGLISH], they marched into the factory and the guards went all the way around, and we were inside free.

But in that camp-- in that plant, there were working Russian prisoners of war, French prisoners of war, English prisoners of war, so many Poles with the the P on what they were forced to come-- and then they had [NON-ENGLISH] on it, Russians.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I worked there for-- they put me in a kommando, also, low voltage for Siemens-Halske.
I worked on telephones.
Yes.
When they take us--
Yes, we were standing.
Appell, yes.
We had to--
We locked arms.
We marched, we marched into the plant.
Into the plant.
And the guards went to the outside of the plant.
We marched inside the plant.
And each kommando had a different trade.
We was in-- I was in the electrical kommando.
So we marched into the electrical shack, where the construction was over there, to, like, a company.
Siemens-Halske was the name.
Siemens-Halske was like a subcontractor for the telephone company.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I remember that in the beginning over there, there were all balloons around the plant.
And later on, when the airplanes came, they started to take away the balloons.
I don't know if they shot them down, or-- there were not any balloons.
But in every intersection there was like an incinerator, and then there was an alarm.
They turned on the incinerator, so it smoked up the whole plant with smoke-- that the enemy should not see what it is in there, in that plant.
And there was an alarm.
